perturbation.original_text: If the Fund's Net Investment Income on any day is below zero, the Investment Adviser shall waive its Advisory Fee or reimburse the Fund an amount (defined as "Expense Waiver") sufficient to produce a Net Investment Income of zero.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Fund's Net Investment Income on any day is below zero, the Investment Adviser may consider waiving its Advisory Fee or reimbursing the Fund an amount (defined as "Expense Waiver").
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the *requirement* for the Investment Adviser to waive fees or reimburse the fund when the Net Investment Income is negative, making it discretionary. This could lead to the fund operating with negative income, impacting shareholders. Registered investment companies like Securian Funds Trust have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of their investors and maintain a certain level of operational solvency.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Investment Company Act of 1940 - Section 36 - Breach of Fiduciary Duty
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 36 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 outlines the fiduciary duties of investment advisers. By making the waiver of fees or reimbursement discretionary when the fund has negative net income, the modified text potentially allows the Investment Adviser to prioritize its own interests over the Fund's and its shareholders', which could be construed as a breach of fiduciary duty. The law requires investment advisers to act in the best interest of the fund, which includes maintaining operational solvency.

Breach of fiduciary duty (a) Civil actions by Commission; jurisdiction; allegations; injunctive or other relief The Commission is authorized to bring an action in the proper district court of the United States, or in the United States court of any territory or other place subject to the jurisdiction of the United States, alleging that a person who is, or at the time of the alleged misconduct was,
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Investment Adviser to waive fees or reimburse the fund to maintain a zero Net Investment Income, while the changed text makes this action discretionary. This directly contradicts the fiduciary duty outlined in Section 36 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (as understood from the Law Explanation) which requires investment advisers to act in the best interest of the fund and maintain operational solvency. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 only mentions 'Breach of fiduciary duty' and doesn't detail the specifics, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 only maps the Investment Company Act of 1940 to its corresponding USC Title and Section, the omission of a mandatory protection creates a legal contradiction. By removing the obligation, the changed text introduces a scenario where the fund could operate with negative income, potentially harming shareholders, which is not in their best interest.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall continue in effect for a period of one year from the date of its execution and from year to year thereafter provided such continuance is specifically approved by a majority of the trustees of the Trust who (i) are not "interested persons" of the Trust or any other party to this Agreement, as defined in the 1940 Act, and (ii) have no direct or indirect financial interest in the operation of this Agreement ("Non- Interested Trustees").
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue in effect for a period of one year from the date of its execution and from year to year thereafter provided such continuance is specifically approved by a majority of the trustees of the Trust.
perturbation.explanation: The omission removes the requirement that the trustees approving the agreement's continuation must be 'Non-Interested Trustees,' as defined by the 1940 Act. This means that trustees with a conflict of interest (e.g., financial ties to the Investment Advisor) could approve the continuation, violating the spirit and intent of the Investment Company Act of 1940, designed to protect investors from conflicts of interest.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Investment Company Act of 1940 - Section 15(c) - Approval of investment advisory contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that the board of directors (or trustees) of a registered investment company, including a majority of independent directors, specifically approve the investment advisory contract. By removing the condition that the approving trustees be 'Non-Interested Trustees,' the modified text allows for the possibility that interested parties could unduly influence the approval, undermining investor protection.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has been approved by the vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such registered company, and— (1) precisely describes all compensation to be paid thereunder; (2) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company; (3) provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, by the board of directors of such registered company or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser ; and (4) provides, in substance, for its automatic termination in the event of its assignment . (b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'Non-Interested Trustees' requirement directly contradicts the Investment Company Act of 1940's intent to protect investors from conflicts of interest. By removing this safeguard, the modified text allows trustees with a direct financial interest to approve the agreement's continuation, which could lead to decisions that benefit the trustees themselves rather than the trust's beneficiaries. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15, emphasizes the necessity of contracts being approved to protect the company, a protection that is undermined by the omission.

perturbation.original_text: If the Investment Adviser is required to reimburse the Fund, the Investment Adviser shall make such payment within 30 days after each month-end in the amount due the Fund as of each month.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Investment Adviser is required to reimburse the Fund, the Investment Adviser shall make such payment at its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the legally required 30-day payment term for reimbursements from the Investment Advisor to the Fund. Registered investment companies like Securian Funds Trust are expected to follow certain financial guidelines and timetables to benefit stakeholders. Without the 30-day rule, this agreement violates the law and introduces uncertainty around when the fund will receive its payments. Funds and investors would not be able to ascertain income accurately.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Investment Company Act of 1940 - Section 22c-1 and Rule 22c-1 - Pricing of Redeemable Securities for Distribution, Redemption, and Repurchase
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 22(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 and Rule 22c-1 mandate fair pricing of fund shares, which depends on accurate net asset value (NAV) calculations. By eliminating the 30-day payment deadline, the modified agreement introduces ambiguity and potential delays in reimbursement payments to the Fund, which directly affects the funds calculations and valuation. This could result in inaccurate NAV calculations, violating the requirement for fair pricing and leading to potential investor harm.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a mandatory 30-day payment term with discretionary payment timing for reimbursements. This contradicts the principle of fair pricing of fund shares, as accurate NAV calculations are jeopardized by potential delays in reimbursement payments, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 17 CFR 270.22c-1, which is related to rules and regulations under the Investment Company Act of 1940, and the Law Explanation discusses Section 22(c) of this act. The modification removes a key financial guideline (30-day rule) and could lead to inaccurate NAV calculations, potentially harming investors.
